title: MAX_DATABASE_CONNECTIONS env var silently falls back to 1 on parse failure
body: Database::new in src/database.rs reads MAX_DATABASE_CONNECTIONS via std::env::var(...).unwrap_or_else(...).parse().unwrap_or(1), so setting MAX_DATABASE_CONNECTIONS=abc or a negative number just quietly becomes a pool size of 1 with only a debug-level log line, which under real hbbs load looks like a mysterious connection bottleneck rather than a config typo.

title: Database::new creates an empty sqlite file even if create_tables subsequently fails
body: Database::new() in database.rs does std::fs::File::create(url).ok() before setting up the pool and calling create_tables(), so on a failed migration (e.g. disk full, or existing file with an incompatible schema) the empty/partial .sqlite3 file is left behind and a naive restart sees the file already exists, skipping fs::File::create and hitting the corrupt-schema path directly.

title: IP_BLOCKER and IP_CHANGES maps in peer.rs grow unbounded for high-churn IPs
body: peer.rs defines IpBlockMap and IpChangesMap as lazy_static globals keyed by IP string, tracking (count, Instant) pairs, but I don't see any sweep that removes stale entries once IP_BLOCK_DUR/IP_CHANGE_DUR has elapsed for an IP that stops connecting -- only lookups seem to check expiry, not evict. Under an internet-facing rendezvous server this map can grow indefinitely from scanning bots.

title: Commented-out user/disabled fields on peer::Peer suggest half-finished multi-tenant support
body: src/peer.rs's Peer struct has // pub(crate) user: Option<Vec<u8>> and // pub(crate) disabled: bool commented out right next to the active PeerInfo/reg_pk fields, while src/database.rs's Peer (a different, DB-row struct) does have a live `user: Option<Vec<u8>>` field. This split makes it unclear whether per-peer user association / disabling is actually wired up end-to-end or only partially implemented.

title: docker/healthcheck.sh and docker/rootfs/usr/bin/healthcheck.sh have diverged
body: There are two healthcheck scripts, docker/healthcheck.sh and docker/rootfs/usr/bin/healthcheck.sh, which appear to serve the same purpose for the hbbs/hbbr containers but live in different paths; it's not documented which one the Dockerfile actually installs, so a fix applied to one during an incident might not affect the image actually being shipped.
